_ CURRENT KFFAIRS,

If the “saved ’’ Commissioners of Rathmines have a
conscientious objection to work of any kind on the Sab-
bath, perhaps they would shut off the electric supply
altogether on Sundays, and those who depend on Rath-
mines electricity for the lighting of their houses could
lay in a supply 6f oil and candles for one day in the
weck. However, the Rathmines Commissioners supply
the light, so the diminished quantity of it that answers
to the switch on Sunday in comparison with week days
cannot have anything to do with conscientious scruples
of Mr. Edmundson and his gallant !)and of “saved.”
Might we suggest, if it is not lése-majesté even to ‘make
a suggestion to the promoters of the recontly-rojected
Bill, that if they cannot give a decent supply of light
on Sundays, that they should rqake arrangements to
divide the constituency into five mstm.ul of the present
two wards, and so create an opportunity for something
like fair represenb&ticn of the ratepayers on the Council.

It is not because Mr. Edmundson and his band of
“saved ”’ may like to feel gloomy on the Sabbath day
that customers of the municipal electric supply must be
content with a misorable supply of electric light in their
homes on Sundays.

Perhaps it is not to be wondered at that a discussion
about coal should give rise to heat. At a recent meet-
ing of the Granard Board of Guardians tenders for a
supply of forty tons of coal were considered. The ad-
vertisoment specified “ Whitehaven Screened Coal,”’” but
notwithstanding this, the Arigna Mining Co., Ltd.,
sent in a tender for their own Irish coal. The Irish coa!
was tendered at 19s. 2d., the other at 23s. 3d. Mr
Masterson proposed that the Irish coal be accepted
It was used, he stated, in the Convent at Longford, in
the Longford Workhouse, and in the Multyfarnham
Friary. Apart from any other consideration, Mr. Mas-
terson urged that it should be taken on the ground of
economy. The Master of the Workhouse spoke strongly
against the quality of the Irish coal, though Mr. Master-
son very reasonably asked, by way of reply, why they
used the coal in Longford Workhouse, and in the Friary
at Multyfarnham. Eventually Mr. Masterson’s propo-
sition was ruled out of order, as the advertisement spe-
cified Whitehaven screened coal. What have the Arigna
Coal people to say to the reflections on the quality of
their product? By the way, is it not practicable to
place the coal on the Dublin market, and so give an op-
portunity to coal users in the metropolis to test its
quality ?

Truly there is nothing new under the sun. The editor
of this paper never thought that the barbarous com-
pound Ballytown which he used in a book of his had ever
been coined before. Yet here is a reader sending us up
an extract which he took from some publication or other
in which the word not only occurs, but from which it
appears that there is or was actually such a place. Here
is the extract : — The ‘ Bangalore,” commanded by Cap-
tain Lynch, sailed from the island of Amboyna on the
3vd of April, 1802, bound to Ballytown, in the Island
of Lomback, and from thence to Batavia.’’

Our suggestion last week that Lady Aberdeen would
give a great impetus to Irish industry if at the next
open-air function that she organised she let it be under-
stood that she would be dressed and ornamented alto-
gether with articles of Irish manufacture and that she
wished all her guests to follow her lead is, weo learn, not
only not a new one, but it appears that twenty years ago
Lady Aberdeen actually did something of the sort. We
hear that it then created something like consternation
amongst the women concerned. It was harder to dress
in Irish manufactured stuff then than now. By the
way someone has gone to the trouble to look through the
descriptions published in the press of dresses worn by the
ladies who attended the recent drawingroom, and out of
76 described as wearing lace only 33 wore Irish lace;
tho balanco of 43 wore foreign lace. There’s practical
patriotism for you! Wa venture to think that there
were more of the up-start class amongst the forty-three
than amongst the thirty-three. An Irish “ fashionable’’
upstart would be inclined, no doubt, to look upon even
Irish lace as “ vulgaw.”

The following statement, which has been sent us over
the signatures of Mr. James Coter, captain, and Mr.
Charles Leonard, secretary of the Markethill (Co. Ax-
magh) Gaelic Football Club, throws a vivid light on the
“toleranca’’ of the “Saved ’’ in that part of the coun-
try :—On last September a Gaelic football club was
started in Markethill. The members having practised
on Sundays, it caused some uneasiness of conscience to

‘our Protestant neighbours.  But though they talked
about and reprobated the practice still they showed no
signs ef opposition until the night of November 7th,
1905, when the Ordnance Survey Corps (20 in number
and Catholics) left the town. On that night somo of
our “tender conscienced ’’ Loyalists proceeded to the
field and broke and carried away the goal and point
posts. The attention of tha police had been called to
the matter, and they made a pretended search, but were
unable to find the posts. About a fortnight afterwards
the posts were found in the yard of a Protestant farmer
who lived a short distance from tho football field—
which fact shows the active and vigilant polico we have
in Markethill.  After this matters went on smoothly
until Januavy 1st, 1906, when the club took a new field
from the Misses Hagan. Then threats were used against
these two lone creatures because they gave a field to tho
Gaelic Club. A kind of boycott was inaugurated, and
no Protestant would sell or give them milk. The climax
was reached on the night of the 2nd inst., whon a num-
ber of persons strewed the whole football ficld (about 3
acres) with broken glass. It was sown like oats over the
whole field, thereby rendering it useless either for graz-
ing or football. Besides on the night of the 3rd inst.
(Saturday) threatening notices werc posted up on the
road leading to the Catholic Church. Besides the usual
cries of the Loyalists they contained the following:—
“ The captain (whoss name was given) is doomed.”” “ To
h—— Pope.”  “ Whoever helps the Hagans will be
slashed (probably meant shot).”” Now you see that tho
Catholics are in the minority here and hence they can
trample on us as they like. "But as long as we had the
Survey Corps here they did not attempt to interfers with
us. I think thisoutrage deserves publicity, and I trust
you will afford space for a sharp comment on it in your
valuable journal. Signed on bzhalf of the members of
the Markethill Gaelic Football Club.—James Cotter,
Captain ; Charles Leonard, Sec.”’

“Gipe 50 bpag! is surely not niore out of place on the
notes of the so-called ‘‘ National’’ Bank than ‘‘ Cead
mille Failthe *’ (the spelling is not ours) on the pro-
gramme of a ‘“ Grand Christy Minstrel Entertainment’’
on, of all nights in the year, £t .Patrick’s Night, at
Cahir, in ‘“ gallant ”’ Tipperary. Cahir, God help us!
Our readers, no doubt, retain lively impressions of a
vigorous article of Cahir of the ‘‘ Milintary ’ that ap-
peared some months ago in the LEapEr. This ‘‘ Cead
Mille Failthe ** Christy Minstrel atrocity was held, not
only on St. Patrick’s Day, but it was held in the Con-
vent School, and in aid of the St. Vincent de Paul
Society! Some funny people, we believe, attempt to
make out that St. Patrick was a Protestant! Do the
Christy Minstrels of Cahir, the authorities of the Con-
vent School, and the local St. Vincent de Paul Society
advance the proposition that St. Patrick was—a Coon !
Mr. Lonergan, according to the programme, sang ““Why
can’t a Coon wed a young white girl.”” We are not
clever enough to attempt to answer that conundrum;
but we might ask: ““ Why can’t Mr. Lonergan, whoever
he is, sing an appropriate song on St. Patrick’s Night ?”’
In Dublin St. Patrick’s Night was to a large extont Mr.
Bung’s Night; in Cahir it evidently was the Coons’
Night. A Mr. J. Cooke sang ‘‘ The moon, the coon,
and the little octoroon.””  Perhaps, on next Coon’s
Night, 17th March, 1907, Mr. Cooke would sing ‘“ The
coon, the cook and the little peoinin’ for a change.

In the course of a lefter to us Liam O Docapcary
writes: —‘‘ Unfortunately, the need that exists for an
Act of Parliament for the compulsory closing of all
publichouses on festivals like St. Patrick’s Day was
only too painfully evident on Saturday night. Passing
from one side of the city to the other, T found the
majority of the publichouses open, and full of that class
of the community to whom the ‘‘guzzling’’ of intoxi
cating fluids secems the ‘‘ be-all and end-all”” of their
existence. And in almost every street through which
one passed were to be met the sbupid-looking, staggering
patrons of these low-class beer-saloons—not ~drunk
enough to swell the police statistics, but quite drunk

- onough to bring disgrace upon the celebration of the

Fostival of our Patron Saint.”

Mr. Patrick Crotty, draper, Main Street, Lismore,
writes stating that the drapers of that town had no-
thing to do with the dance to which we referred in a
recent issue, and abt which “ Union Jacks’’ streamed,
and a Tommy Atkins band from Waterford played
“God Save the King.”

Undler tho heading *“ An Irvish Literary Fovce,” the
following appreciation of the Lrapen appears in a ro-
cent number of 7'e Austral Light, an influential Aus-
tralian monthly magazine : —

The Dublin LeADER is, according lo ils prospectus, “a roviow
of politics, 1 terature, art, and industry.” It is edited by a gentle-
man of solid attainments and great litorary ability, whose courage,
balance, and splendid common senso are amongat the most refresh.
ing features of Irish journalism. As regular contributors it has a
number of gifted writers, strongly endowed with froshness and
force. That br:lliant and unanswerablo work on ‘“ Catholicity and
Progress in Ireland” originally appaared in its columns.  Its
author, the Rev. Dr. M. O'Riordan, after giving to his country
perhaps the most valuable gift she has received from any ividual
for over a generation, has been appointed Rector of the Irish Col
lu_ge at Rome, a positon in which he has been preceded by many
distinguished men. Tho Leabkr editor and his colleagues have
made their paper a big force in Ireland, and have done, and arc
doing, much for nationality, as expre'sod in language, litcrature,
industries, customs, etc., and as distinct from, and fuller than, mere
political nationality. It is al:o a powerful antagonist of the ascend-
ancy caste in Ireland, and battles ceaselessly for fair play for
Catholic Irishmen in their own country, not by mere declamation,

= but by trenchant exposure of the ““jobs’’ put up on those pcople,
and the shameless manner in which they are boycotted out of all
positions of emolument in publc and privale en'erprises. The
exposure of the railway and banking systems in this regard was
ruthlessly carried out to the smallest detail. Now we have before
us, under the heading “ Belfast and Bigotry,” the list of Corpora-
dion officers and employees of that city for 1905.

Most of the combs bought in Ireland to-day come
from Scotland and Germany. One Scotch comb manu-
facturing firm gives employment to 2,000 hands in
Aberdeen. At one time, when the Irish people used
Irish-made combs, there were 20 manufacturers in
Dublin alone, and over 4,000 people engaged in the in-
dustry throughout the country. Messrs Mitchell,
to whom we referred last week, are now the only manu-
facturers in Ireland. The combs manufactured by this
Irish firm are made entirely of horn. Owing to the in-
troduction of the short-horn breed of cattle Irish horn
is less used than formerly, and South America now fur-
nishes the bulk of the raw material, but the Irish horn
is still suitable for, and largely employed in, certain
classes of combs. Our representative, who went through
Messrs, Mitchell’s factory in Dublin, has shown us a
specimen of their work which stiikes us as being of ex-
cellent, quality and finish.

Male labour is chiefly employed, we arc informed, in
-this branch of Irish manufacture, but the lighter and
finishing processes are done by girls. Messrs. Mitchell
have had from time to time to import skilled
labour from Scotland through a scarcity of the Irish
material, and we are glad to learn that their experience
teaches them that the Irish workman is more reliable.
At present, all the hands employed are Irish, and have
been trained in their own factory. Our readers will be
inberested to hear that a large quantity of Irish-manu-
factured combs for stable purposes are exported to Tng-
land every year; but, of course, the great bulk of the
firm’s output consists of toilet or dressing combs. The
latter sell retail at prices ranging from a penny to
2s. 6d., and all are stamped with the lettering,  Irish
made.”” Weo have heard of unstamped combs heing
hawked around to private houses in Dublin, and sold to
the occupants as Irish manufacture, and we should like
to make it known that such articles are not at all what

~ they are professed to be.

Apart from the immediate injury done to an Irish in-
dustry by this Daddy Swindle-fingers practice, it has
Ixzen tha cause of much indirect harm. W are in-
ormed that many people who had inferior foreign

Trinity College was literally spellbound by the mild
criticism to which His Grace the Archbishop of Dublin
treated it. Tho speech was delivered on a Friday and
the spell lasted on tho notorious spelling University for
nearly a wock, At length tho dazed university put up
one of its highly-paid professors to reply, and a letter
appeared in the daily papers of the following Thursday.
Lord Bung’s Bapress and Alf Fox then sharpened their
pencils and wrote leading articles in defence of the spell-
bound University. Ts it not a curious thing that neither
the Hzpress nor Alf Fox went into the leading article
lino against His Giraco’s specch until ono of the profes-
gors of the spellbound university plucked up courage to
writo a letter.

His Grace was laid up in bed and so the reason for the
absence of a prompt and crushing reply to Professor
Dixon of the Souper(ior) University was obvious. In
Tuesday’s papers, however, though His Grace is yot on
the sick list, the mud-throwing university has got its
reply. We watch this controversy with great interest.
Traill had better call up all hands on deck; even the
sonneteer may be useful, and the gallant mud-throwers
- who at times climb the railings should be ready for any
emergency.

Mud-throwing probably comes casier to some of the
“ Trinity ”’ cads than spelling.  Several students of
this discredited College distinguished themseclves on
Saturday last by throwing oggs, pellets, and bags of
flour at the Lord Mayor’s carriage. The nature of the
missiles show clearly that the mud-throwing black-
guards and curs of “Trinity ’’ had made careful pre-
parations. We have not seen any letters in the papers
from Lord Meath about the blackguardism of these un-
mannerly curs. These  gentlemen’’ can do what
they like; the whites of sanctimonious eyes only look
aloft with horror if a few “ Idolatrous’’ students object
to the tune which, in practice, means “ Damn the Na-
tionalists.”” Well, pitching eggs and things at a Lord
Mayor’s carriage will come easier to the ill-mannered
cads of Trinity College than answering the indictment
of the Archbishop. Professor Dixon cannot dispose of
the Archbishop with rotten eggs; he will have to try
his head at logic. We wonder did any of the Nutting
blacklegs distinguish themselves in the characteristic
and intellectual display of Saturday last.

of sly dogs, their little Bill got a nasty kick-out from
the Imperial Parliament. See what sometimes happens
even though we have not Home Rule! It will be re-
membered that old Mr. Edmundson was Chairman at the
ratepayers’ meeting that was called at ten o’clock one
raw November morning in connection with the promo-
tion of this Bill. Old Mr. Edmundson and his friends
were in a tight corner, for the expenses incurred by the
previous unsuccessful attempt to run a Bill through had
to be met, and we understand there was a clause in the
Bill that was kicked out last week empowering the Com-
missioners to pay the expenses of the previous Bill out
of the rates. The necessary ratepayers’ meeting was ar-
ranged as quietly as possible. Even such an alert man
as Mr. Michael Sullivan, who lives in the district, only
heard of it by chance, and the hour was fixed at 10
o’clock in the morning. However, notwithstanding all

Sir,—Will you allow me to ask some of the Gaelic
scholars among the readers of the LEADER to suggest
some Irish name for a motor boat that I am having built
for cruising on the upper Mississippi River and northern
lakes. It should be stimulating to the revivalists to
think that the light has spread so far, and that through
the mighty valley of the Mississippi River and upon the
great northern lakes a modern motor boat will be known
by some beautiful Irish name.

The name should be given in Roman characters. It
should also be short, and, of course, melodious.

I wonder what the name for “ Flying Fish ’’ is in
Irish. Some such name as the “ Water Fly,”” or “ Dra-
gon Fly,”” or a bird that darts and moves with greab
rapidity, would be suitable.

Putting the name in Roman characters is not to evade
the enlightened legislation of Great Britain upon.the
subject of Irish names, but because I think it would be
useful to print the name in characters that would allow
people uneducated in Gaelic to pronounce it.—Respect-
fully yours, JorN D. O’BRIEN.

WANTED—A BRITISH BILL TO BUNG BUNG.

UNG, of Dublin, to a great extent, defied patriotic

3 opinion, kept his Bond Slaves working at full
pressure on the day that, according to Mr. Robert Rus-
sell, ““ has come to be recognised as a National Holiday
in Ireland,” and sponged right profitably on St. Pat-
rvick. It is quite evident that the only way to shut up
Patriotic Dublin Bung on St. Patrick’s Day is by
means of an Act of the British Parliament.

Bung, of Dublin, snaps his fingers at patriotic
opinion, and treats with scorn the exhortations of his
Grace the Archbishop. Bung did nothing to make St.
Patrick’s Day a recognised holiday, but others having
exerted themselves successfully to that end, bad Bung
proceeds like the bad landlords to reap where he has
not sown. His Bond Slaves are kept at work harder
than ever because others have made the day a holiday,
and his tills rattle with the pennies of his large army
of dupes who spend on drink what they should exchange
for food and clothing for their families.

Bung is a great “Irish Patriot.”” We saw Daniel
Tallon, keeper of the magic button, outside his open
bungery on St. Patrick’s Day; the doors of Alderman
Delahunt’s two bungeries were open; Mooney’s, in
Abbey Street, was also open, and the world at home
and abroad knows that one of the directors of Mooney’s
is no less a person than a real, live ‘ Nationalist’
M.P.—young Mr. Mooney, who ran away from South
Dublin, and had himself foisted into Newry. We
have no authentic account of the number of Dublin
public-houses that werce closed, but as far as we saw
the closed houses were on Saturday last very much the
exceptions.

So long as Bung sponges on St. Patrick, it would, in
many respects, be better—so far as many a family is
concerned—that the old arrangement had not been
upset, and that work went on as heretofore ~ Many
a bread-winner for a family loses one day’s wages, and
he spends another loafing about from Tallon’s to Dela-
hunt’s, and from Delahunt’s to Mooney’s, and from
Mooney's to Alderman Davin’s, and so on. If such a
man had been working he would not only be the richer
by his day’s wages, but also the richer by the amount
which he would not have spent loafing about adding
coppers and sixpenny pieces to the fund out of which
the silks and satins and coster-monger feathers that
the women-folk of “the lower and less reputable
class '’ Bungs are wont to adorn themselves with in
their foolish and vain efforts to masquerade as fine

ladies.

A correspondent who passed from one side of the
oity to the other on Mr. Bung’s night—after all, why
continue to cheat Bung of the fame that is his due by
calling it St. Patrick’s night +—writes—“ And in almost
every street through which one passed were to be met
the stupid-looking, staggering patrons of these low-class
beer saloons—not drunk enough to swell the police sta-
tistics, but quite drunk enough to bring disgrace upon
the celebration of tho Festival of our Patron Saint.’’

It is only fair to note that the publicans showed more
patriotism in some other parts of Ireland than in Dub-

_lin. TFor instance, in Limerick we read that out of over
three hundred public-houses only two or three were
open. Certainly, Limerick is to be congratulated on
having so few of that class of ““lower and less reput-
able ’ publicans who defy Ireland by sponging on St

. Patrick. *

The experience of those years sinco the effort to
honour St. Patrick’s Day in a worthy manner was
begun, all leads up to tho conclusion that the only satis-
factory solution of the publican question is a Bill of
the British Parliament, enacting that all drink-shops be
bunged up for that day. Of course, it is a “ humiliat-
ing reflection,” to use the phrase of Bung’s sccretary,
upon the character of a class of Irish people that a
large number of the publicans cannot be induced by
patriotic sentiment to refrain from sponging on St. Pat-
rick, but one must look facts in the face ~Nothing but
an  Act of the Britash Parliament will induce
“ patriots”’ like Messrs. Mooney, Tallon, Delahunt,
Davin, and the like to fall into patriotic line in the
way of honouring St. Patrick. The need mnow is a
British Bill to bung Bung.

A NEW POETS WORK.*

NEW poet does not emerge every day, though
A new verse-writers make their appearance, un-
asked, as thick as sparrows, and much the same in
singing power. Mr. Thomas Boyd is a new poet, and
as such his little volume of twenty-five poems must be
weighed with care. Mr. Boyd is a poet, and whether
he hits or misses in any one poem, he is always a poet,
and not a mere verse-turner.  As for his faculty, it
is almost exclusively lyrical, so far as this volume is
eoncerned. His subjects are not so easily summed up
in one word; they are various rather than numerous.
The old Celtic mythology is largely represented, both
directly and allusively, and we have a few ballads, and

A a few personal poems. In manner Mr. Boyd is new,
rather than novel; the temperament, the psychic ele-
ment, rather than the “form,”” is new. There is no
hint of Moore or Davis here. At times Mr. Boyd
seems to recall Mangan; imitate him, or for that
matter anybody else, he does not. At times there is
a suggestion of Swinburnian influence in the technique,
as in the final stanza of “ Banba,”” which has several
lines that remind one of the manner of some of the
choruses in “ Atalanta in Calydon.”” But I only sug-
gest that Mr. Boyd may have been influenced at points
by Swinburne; not that he has ever imitated him. I
would not say that he owes anything to Mr. Yeats,
for Mr. Boyd’s line has far more nerve and firmness
than the line of his compeer—if, indeed, Mr. Boyd be
not something more than the compeer of My. Yeals.
As for his national views, they will be sufficiently in-
dicated by the vigorous conclusion of “ Banba’’:—

‘“Thou hearest, as from afar,

The golden thunders. of war,

The cries of thy conquering lords,

The ringing, the singing of Swards

That shall crown thee, O Banba!’’

These lines have a distinctly Swinburnian ring; in the
remainder of his volume the English pooct’s influenco is
much less traceable—in most places not at all. M.
Boyd is really “his own man.”

One difficulty in judging cortain of these poems arises
out of the nature of the subjects. I suppose. it must
‘be my own fault, but I am not sufficiently an adoept in,

our old mythology and Celtic legendary lore to he

“The shouting peoples of the air,
The dark tribes of the glen,

The agony of men.” 4
“Cliach "’ is a live poem, indeed.  But it has many
companions in vitality. ~ “Clan Lir,”” in which Mr.
Boyd shows himself an artist in rhythm, is one of
these.  Another is the “Death of Cuchulainn.”
“TLove on the mountain’’ is a beautiful little lyric,
and “I Bring a Sword”’ is a thing of power. The
four picces in ballad form are all good, but “ The Ston-
ing of Darragh’ is the strongest of them. * Ethne
in Torinis’’ contains much poetical beauty besides
showing Mr. Boyd’s handling of the heroic couplet; it
contains passages worthy of Keats, such as that begin-
ning “ Often I gazed.” These twelve lines have both

“ The earth shall teach thee, and the wandering air
The deep things of the world lay bare;
Thou shalt have gladness in the light, -
And to thy slumber dreams shall throng—
Dreams from the starry legions of the night,
And slumber lulled by planetary song.”

In the “ Leanan Sidhe’’ I find this:—
“Thy lovely motions, answering to the rime ]
That ancient Nature sings, ea

That keeps the stars in cadence for all time 3

And echoes through all things.” ﬁ

“The Heath’’ and “ The King’s Son’’ are good ; in espe- 4

cial the latter. In “ Batt Scanlan, Fiddler,”” Mr. Boyd
celebrates an Irishman’s true joy in his native music—
a joy only to be tasted to the full, so far as Irish music
is concerned, by a Celt.
“You bring us laughter, bring us tears,
And visions of our land;
Exiled, wo lose the fret of years,
All Ireland in your hand.
The cloud that hides the past departs—
And what is this you give?
The fire, the passion of their hearts,
‘Who died that we may live.””
Further on we get a fine line:—
“The deeps of listening awr.”’

Another lyric colebrates the same topic, this time in
honour of an Irish girl-singer.
“Sho tells of ancient, simple things
That never can grow old; 5
Of dawn, and May-time blossomings,

I seg the silver sallows shake

The singer was heard in London, which fact added to
the appeal of the music.  Striking lines we meet in
plenty. Thus in “ Leanan Sidhe’’ :—

“ Shutting behind him Life’s last gloomy door.””

In “On the Road to the Ford’ are many fine things,
had T but time to quote them. In “Clan Lir” we
s‘eeln‘to get a symbol of Ireland’s fate—and hope. The
“ Evil Woman*’ in that poem I take to be perfidious
Albion.
“She has taken from us

Our form and our beauty,

The use of our being

And the land of our love;

Only our voices

Ave left us for singing

In Western waters

OQur doom and our loss.”

Yet we are told that the Evil Woman has left us one
hope: —
“That while our voices
Sound the sweet Gaelic,
The sweet human Gaelic,
Our nature is ours;
And again shall we enter
The place of our people,
Renewed in our being
"In form as in song.”’

I think the ear is rather disappointed of an expected
rhyme in the fourth and eighth lines of each stanza in
this beautiful lyric. But no matter; it remains beau-
tiful.

I take the “Donn Cuailgne’ poem to be Mr.
Boyd’s master-piece in classically strong, nervous verse.
I quote it in full.

“ The sons of gods have died for him,

The sons of mortals die;

He breathes death from his nostrils’ rim,
Death lightens from his eye.

Slowly he journeys to the West
Trampling the sons of light;

Beneath his hooves their limbs are pressed
Red in the wastes of night.

The shadows of the primal wars
Darken his giant sides,
And his harness gleams with glimmering stars
That light his mighty strides.
His voice is of the Deep; his path
‘With the fair dead is strewn;
Awful, upon his brow he hath
The great horns of the moon.”

Yet I dare
say it will never find its way into any of the English
Anthologies. Take up even Palgrave’s almost too
famous  Golden Treasury.”” There you shall find
things like Wordsworth’s “ Simon Lee '’ admitted, and
things like Prior’s “ The merchant, to securc his frea-
sure.”” A surplusage of “Yarrow,” too, finds place
there, but—not a syllable of Mangan or Davis! No,
not one. And who knows what recognition Mr. Boyd
will ever get in such quarters? Well, no matter. This
is surely all the more, not the less, reason why we who
are his own countrymen should give him recognition, so
far as in our power lies. He himself celebrates poor
Batt Scanlan, the fiddler:—

“ A deeper night is drawing nigh
To darken human fame:
The lights go out, and all things die;

But here lives on your name.’’

By AN INSURANCE MANAGER.

BLUE Book was issued recently containing re-

turns relating to Life Insurance Companies.

Ixcluding the figures for colonial and foreign com-

panies and sickness and friendly societics, it shows that

the policies in force in Great Britain and Ireland are:—

Number. Amount insured.
Ordinary 2,303,422 £737,380,494
Industrial 24,668,532 £241,869,649

Just pause, and think of it for a moment!—nearly
27,000,000 contracts and £1,000,000,000 insurances—
and let us Irishmen ask ourselves, how long do we in-
tend to permit the Saxon and the Scot to completely
monopolise so great and growing a business right in our
midst? Ts not our present position exceedingly humi-
liating ?

Life Insurance, year after year, grows larger and
larger in every country, and deservedly so; for it en-
courages habits of thrift, forethought, and self-
reliance. It strengthens the home, and provides a
means by which the family, after the bread-winner’s de-
cease, may be brought up without the stigma of pau-
perism to haunt, handicap and humiliate them during
their struggles through life. But, to Ireland, Life In-
surance is a foreign institution, and until that “is
changed by the establishment of a Mutual Life Office,
managed and controlled by Irishmen, insurance agencies
must, I fear, be classed with agencies for emigration and
for absentee landlords.

It is, indeed, gratifying to find letters coming from
all parts of Ireland promising hearty support for the
proposed new Irish venture, and stating how much in-
surance the writers would be prepared to effect in it.
The spirit is certainly abroad, but much will depend
on the promises of support that we shall receive. The
undertaking must be a success from the commencement,
and of that there will not be the slightest doubt if in-
tended policy-holders will write, as so many-have been
doing lately, and place me in the position of showing
that they “mean business.”” All who write me should
also speak to their friends, and request them to do like-
wise. The country is ripe for an Irish Insurance Com-
pany that shall be owned solely by those who insure
in it.

Several letters which have been received, promising
support to the proposed Irish Co., it is pleasing to note,
are from the clergy. If space permitted I would quate
largely from these letters, but let the following serve as
a fair specimen of their tenor:—‘“T feel perfectly con-
fident that every clergyman in Ireland, not already in-
sured, would patronise the Irish Mutual Insurance Com-
pany, and even those who are already insured would, if
means permitted, do likewise. Sixty students
will be ordained' at Maynooth next June. I am con-
vinced that insurances could be effected, amongst, them
alone, to the extent of £10,000.”” The writer addé that
he himself is already insured, but he is prepared to in-
sure i the new Irish Co. also, and pay £5 a year for
his policy. Insurance is certainly an enterprise in tho
promotion: of whioh' the Hierarchy and clergy could
readily take a direct and leading part.  Mosf of the
young priests now-a-days insure, and if the sixty re-
forred to above, insured only for £200 each the new
Co. would thereby receive, at once, an impetus of
£12,000 worth of new business. o

Members of municipal, county and district councils
‘and other sublic bodies have written favourably of the
“project and guaranteed their quota towards he pre-
mium income required for a beginning. As one corres-
pondent says—*‘ Let us all continue to push the matter
ahead and not tire of doing so.”’

One word regarding the encouraging letter of your
correspondent, “‘ Imprimatur,” which appeared last
week. T need only say that Industrial Insurance, which
is so largely availed of by the working classes, will not
be overlooked—neither will the other forms of insur-
ance—when the new company is in a position, with
every element of safety, to transact such business.

I have before me a copy of the Industrial Prospectus
used in Ireland for a company that does a large busi-
ness here. T also have a copy of its corresponding Pros-
pectus for the other side of the Channcl. Higher pre-
miums are charged in the Ivish Prospectus. As long
as we allow owrselves to be bled by the foreigner, what
can we expect ?

Every letter coming to hand offering support is, of
course, regarded as strictly confidential. There is no
Irishman or Irishwoman who cannot take a hand in
launching the proposed much-needed entevprise for
Ireland.

In a swelter of visible -shams posing as Irish,

thoso countries I have named? If he was, why did he
not try to find the truth, and tell it? If he knew the
state of affairs, then he wilfully concealed it from the
public to whom he pretended to tell the truth—for the
rost I leave Mr. Davitt to the mercy of the reader’s
common sense.

In Belgium, the Communes control the schools. That
was not the arrangement of the Belgian people. The
Liberals (i.c., Freemasons) got a hold of the reins. of
government in 1830 when Belgium became free. The
Catholics had to fight their way bit hy bit.  From
1879 to 1884 the Liberals who were in power made a
desporate effort to banish God from the schools. Frere
Orban, their leader, disowned any intention of injur-
ing religion; he only wanted to purify and improve it
in Belgium.  Well, the people hurled them from
power, and the Catholics have ruled the destinies of Bel-
gium ever since, with what success everybody knows.
I have mentioned those facts to point out that what-
over of the secular systems remains in the Belgium
schools is the legacy of the Liberal regime. The Catho-
lics did not alter it more than was essential for prin-
ciple. This is the state of things there. The Com-
munes, as a rule, control the schools, and the State
subsidises them. But there are a great many purely
Catholic schools which are also subsidised by the State.
In the Communal or State schools religious instruction
is part of the day’s education, and is given by the
teacher, or by somebody else under the supervision of
the parish priest. That is the state of affairs in Bel-
gium.  Mr. Davitt, whilst he keeps back all about
Canada, Newfoundland, and Prussia, etc., mentions
Belgium ; and this is how he does it. He says—There
is not, I believe, any religious lessons given by lay
teachers in the public schools in Brussels. The chil-
dren are sent by their parents, if they so desire, to
Church or Chapel on their way to school in the morning
for daily religious instruction.”” Thus he slurs over
Delgium by referring to Brussels only. Now the same
law holds for all Belgium; but in its administration it
is strained in Brussels against the Catholics as much as
the bigotry of the Liberals can carry them; and they
happen to retain their power in Brussels and a few
other large centres where they had concentrated their
anti-Catholic influence in the days of their power.
Hence they man the schools as far as possible with
teachers, not for eflicient teaching power, but accord-
ing to their anti-Catholic spirit. ~Many of those
teachers refuse to give religious instruction; and it is
done by the priests, aided by Catholic lay people. That
is how the exceptional arrangement happens in
Brussels—and Mr. Davitt just tells us of that only.
But even in that little he cannot be accurate. The re-
ligious instruction is given in the school, not in the
Church. Of course, there are many purely Catholic
schools in Brussels also. And that is how Mr. Davitt
enlightens us! But enough of him till next week.

The present holders of all these positions in Ireland,
with an occasional exception, had the one qualification
of being for the most part “ political shoe-blacks,”” elec-

well-known fact in a Western county that a smart young
fellow of 25 runs such an office on a salary of less than
£2 a week.

It is well-nigh time to make a change in these posi-
tions.  They have escaped public attention too long,
and a very large number of people have but the haziest
notion of their existence, still less of their duties and
their salary. A sum of about £60,000 is paid yearly
to such officials in this country, and extremely small
value returned for the money. The matter should at
once bo taken up, and some line of policy pursued by
our M.P.’s, with a view to throwing open these posts by
competitive examination. In these hard times many a
young solicitor with a good University degree would
gladly take £400 a year for doing such work, and this
sum should be the maximum no matter how large the
county. The salaries of present office-holders need re-
